text: On a cold January morning, a research corps boarded a closed vessel bound for waters where dolphins converge. The sender of our orders, an authoritative registrar from the United Kingdom, had a particular and thoughtful plan that left no matter to chance. Each crew member would commute daily between cabins, and oneself alone could not run the ship; a whole troop had to assign duties and admit mistakes agreeably. We had to hasten, for the voyage was unique and the supply of fuel was barely abundant. The captain, my brother, would not gamble with safety, and his respectful manner made even a difficulty seem slightly easier. Notwithstanding the surprising weather, our coverage of the region was duly comparable to any prior survey. We would detect each dolphin by its whisker-like markings and the way it would revolve and distinguish itself from the troop. To recollect every sighting, we kept a recorder running; the camera's projection on a screen helped us literally count each fin. The dairy of the ship's galley smelled of chocolate and a spoonful of cocoa, while a shower of spray would explode over the bow and litter the deck with foam. One instant a raw wound on a sailor's hand needed peroxide and a tranquilizer; the next, an irritate-prone cook would mutter about a phony invoice. We had no time to deserve rest. A weed of doubt could not converge with our resolve, and discomfortable nights notwithstanding, agitation never won. Our usage of new instant tools made the work an elevation of pure oceanography. A particular bullish sponsor, vehement about clean seas, would not consign the project to failure, nor would the violation of any rule go without clarification. The fuse of curiosity, the fertilizer of patience, and a solution of trust kept us united, not disunite. Ashore at last, partly tired, we shouldered each box and cart, paid tribute to the sea, and made our decision: the carbohydrate-rich meals, the kindergarten of young interns, the elevation of every wave were worth it. A pickpocket could steal a wallet, but no thorn, no rifle shot, no aggregation of storms could steal the indebtedness we felt. The agreeably stowage of gear, the closed hold, the can of supplies, all said one thing: a thoughtful crew, sustained by faith, can survive. A protestant chaplain blessed our ball of rope; a kindergarten teacher had once told me a trick: stay humble. Back at the house ashore, we stocked the freezer, watched an old actress on television, and joked that the coast had more arable land than the sea. Though I felt ambivalent about leaving, and our route home was indirect, we said our thanks, and sailed home.
translation: 在一个寒冷的一月清晨，一支研究队登上一艘封闭的船只，驶向海豚汇聚的水域。下达命令的是一位来自英国的权威注册官，他有一个特别而周到的计划，没有把任何事情交给运气。每名船员每天要在舱室之间往返，单凭一个人无法驾驶整船；必须由一整队人分配职责并欣然承认错误。我们必须赶紧，因为这次航行独一无二，而燃料的供应也仅仅勉强充足。船长是我的兄弟，他不会拿安全去赌博，他那恭敬的态度让再大的困难看起来也稍稍容易些。尽管天气出人意料，我们对该区域的覆盖范围理应可与以往任何调查相媲美。我们通过海豚胡须般的斑纹，以及它们旋转、从队群中区分自己的方式来辨认每一只海豚。为了回忆起每一次目击，我们让录音机一直运转；摄像机投射在屏幕上的影像帮助我们逐字逐条数清每一片鳍。船上厨房散发着巧克力和一勺可可的香味，浪花的喷溅会爆发般越过船头，把泡沫撒落在甲板上。前一刻，一名水手手上的鲜血伤口需要双氧水和镇静剂；下一刻，一位易怒的厨师又会嘟囔着抱怨一张假发票。我们没有时间去享受应得的休息。一丝疑虑之草无法与我们的决心汇合，尽管夜晚令人不适，焦躁却从未占上风。我们对新仪器工具的使用让这项工作成为纯粹海洋学的升华。一位特别看涨的赞助人，对清洁海洋充满激情，不会把这个项目交付给失败，任何规则的违反也不会缺少澄清。好奇之引信、耐心之肥料和信任之溶液让我们团结而不分裂。最后上岸时，我们半身疲惫，扛起每一个箱子和推车，向大海致敬，并做出我们的决定：富含碳水化合物的饭菜、年轻实习生的幼儿园、每一道波浪的高度，都是值得的。扒手能偷走钱包，但没有荆棘、没有步枪的枪声、没有风暴的聚合能偷走我们所感到的亏欠之情。装备的妥善堆放、封闭的货舱、那一罐补给，全都说明了一件事：一支周到的船员，在信念的支撑下，能够生存。一位新教随船牧师为我们的绳球祝福；一位幼儿园老师曾告诉我一个诀窍：保持谦卑。回到岸上的房子里，我们把冰柜装满，在电视上看一位年老的女演员演出，还开玩笑说海岸的可耕地比大海还多。尽管我对离开心情矛盾，回家的路线也很迂回，我们还是道了谢，然后扬帆回家。
